title: Need and Demand for Family Planning Among All Women
description: Percentage of all women aged 15 to 49 years with unmet need for family planning, met need for family planning, and percentage of demand satisfied. Source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey.

metricFootnotes.¹: Percentage of demand satisfied is met need divided by total demand.
metricFootnotes.²: Modern methods include female sterilization, male sterilization, IUD, injectables, implants, patch, pill, male condom, female condom, emergency contraception, standard days method (SDM), mucus/Billings/ovulation, basal body temperature, symptothermal, lactational amenorrhea method (LAM), and other modern methods.
metricFootnotes.³: Total demand is the sum of unmet need and met need.

region.psa_note: Region IX includes the province of Sulu in 2025.

notes: ² Modern methods include female sterilization, male sterilization, IUD, injectables, implants, patch, pill, male condom, female condom, emergency contraception, standard days method (SDM), mucus/Billings/ovulation, basal body temperature, symptothermal, lactational amenorrhea method (LAM), and other modern methods.
³ Other methods = total demand for family planning - (unmet need for family planning + modern methods).
“NA” denotes that an estimate is not reported.
1. Numbers in this table correspond to the revised definition of unmet need described in Bradley et al., 2012.
2. Details may not add up to total due to rounding.
Source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2022 and 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey

insights.title: Insights on Need and Demand for Family Planning Among All Women
insights.subtitle: AI-generated insights based on National Demographic and Health Survey data on family planning need and demand among all women
insights.overview: At the national level, total demand for family planning among all women stayed almost unchanged between 2022 and 2025 (41.8 to 41.4), but the method mix inside met need shifted toward modern methods. All-method met need was nearly flat to slightly lower (34.1 to 33.8), while modern-method met need rose (24.3 to 25.7), raising the modern share of met need from about 71.3 percent to 76.0 percent.

sections.heading: National demand stayed stable while modern fulfillment improved
sections.body: The headline demand level changed little (41.8 in 2022 to 41.4 in 2025), and all-method demand satisfied stayed at 81.6 in both years. The bigger change was inside met need: all-method met need edged down (34.1 to 33.8), but modern-method met need increased (24.3 to 25.7). That pushed the modern share of met need from about 71.3 percent to 76.0 percent, indicating substitution toward modern methods rather than a broad expansion of total demand.

sections.heading: Regional gaps are structural, not just ranking noise
sections.body: Regional variation is not only a high-low ranking; regions combine demand and fulfillment in very different ways. BARMM has both the lowest demand (34.8) and by far the highest unmet need (18.5), leaving only 37.4 percent of demand satisfied by modern methods. At the other end, Davao Region (51.3), Caraga (47.7), and SOCCSKSARGEN (45.6) pair high demand with high modern fulfillment (72.2, 67.4, and 71.2), while MIMAROPA records the highest modern demand satisfied (74.5) at a moderate demand level (45.5). Regions like Bicol (55.4) and Central Visayas (59.0) sit much lower on modern fulfillment, showing that demand levels alone do not explain the regional pattern.

sections.heading: Demand rises quickly from young ages and peaks in the late 30s
sections.body: Total demand is lowest at ages 15-19 (6.0) and rises steeply through the 20s and 30s, reaching 63.0 at ages 35-39, before easing at older ages (46.4 at 45-49). Unmet need does not disappear in these high-demand ages: it stays around 9 to 10 points through ages 25-44. This is where the largest volume of unmet need sits because demand itself is high.

sections.heading: Rural women show both higher demand and higher unmet need
sections.body: In 2025, rural demand (44.4) was higher than urban demand (39.1). Rural unmet need was also higher (8.1 vs 7.2), but rural modern met need was notably higher as well (28.8 vs 23.3). Rural modern demand satisfied reached 64.9, above urban 59.6, suggesting that higher rural need is paired with higher rural uptake rather than lower engagement.

sections.heading: Socioeconomic patterns are not a simple straight line
sections.body: By education, demand is highest among women with Grades 1-6 (59.6) and much lower among women with Grades 11-12 (20.4), showing that demand levels vary strongly by life stage and group composition. By wealth, total demand is highest in the lowest quintile (50.7) and lowest in the highest quintile (34.5). Unmet need also declines from lower to higher wealth groups (11.2 in the lowest to 6.1 in the highest), while modern demand satisfied is highest in lower-middle groups and not strictly increasing with wealth.

sections.heading: Age and regional patterns need to be read together
sections.body: The age profile shows where demand pressure is strongest nationally, but regional results show where that pressure is being met and where it is not. High-demand regions can still perform well on modern fulfillment, while low-demand regions can carry disproportionate unmet need. Read together, the two views point to a practical distinction between areas where demand is high because use is already widespread and areas where demand is high because unmet need remains unresolved.

takeaways: Total demand among all women changed little from 2022 to 2025, but modern-method fulfillment improved.
High-demand age groups in the late 20s to early 40s still show unmet need of about 9 to 10 percent, so demand does not automatically translate into use of contraception.
Regional differences are large, and BARMM remains the clearest area where unmet need and low modern fulfillment overlap.

subtitle: Percentage of all women aged 15 to 49 years with unmet need and met need for family planning, and percentage of demand satisfied, according to background characteristics: Philippines, 2022 and 2025
